rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order To Seal Documents
rulings.Rulings: The hearing on this motion will be continued to April 23, 2021, on the court's own motion.

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Special Motion To Strike Second Amended Petition (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: part 4 of 4 Given Keystone's failure to establish that Petitioner's claims against it arise from Keystone's protected activity, the burden would not have shifted to Petitioner to demonstrate a probability of success on its claims. Petitioner is ordered to lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on Thursday, April 1, 2021. Remote appearance is required, whenever feasible, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate.

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Special Motion To Strike Second Amended Petition (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4] The court DENIES this Special Motion to Strike (C.C.P. Section 425.16) Second Amended Petition Under Probate Code Sections 850, 859 as a request for leave to file an untimely motion and STRIKES the motion as untimely and unsupported by sufficient cause to permit late filing. Keystone has failed to establish that its special motion to strike is timely. Keystone seeks an order striking from the Second Amended Petition all allegations against it. The court understands these to be principally that Keystone is holding $50,647.85, representing a portion of the proceeds of the sale of 64 Potomac, and claims that Keystone must account for such funds, return such funds to the estate and pay penalties under Section 859. (See Second Amended Petition, pp. 10-11, 44-46 & pp. 12-13.) These allegations and claims have been in the case since the Initial Petition, which Trammell filed and served in late February 2019. (See Initial Petition at p. 5, 16, 15 [sic], 16 [sic] & p. 7.) The court does not understand Keystone to be moving to strike discrete allegations, given that Keystone failed to quote in its notice any specific allegations. (See CRC rule 3.1322(a).) Perhaps Keystone seeks to strike the allegation that it breached a fiduciary duty owed to Trammell as trust beneficiary when it failed to exercise prudent actions, provide trust information, provide notice of successor trustee, inform beneficiary and provide certain information required under the Probate Code. (See Second Amended Petition, p. 9, 28.) These allegations, including express references to the very same statutory provisions, however, have been in the case since the First Amended Petition, which Trammell filed and served in the summer of 2019. (See First Amended Petition at p. 10, 34.) If Keystone is seeking to strike the allegation that it acted in concert with the other respondents (see Second Amended Petition, p. 11, 47), that allegation has been in the case since the Initial Petition (see Initial Petition at pp. 5-6, 17; see also First Amended Petition, p. 11, 37). Keystone's motion challenges allegations and claims against it that have been present in the case since at least the summer of 2019. Keystone didn't file its anti-SLAPP special motion to strike until November 30, 2020. This motion was not filed within 60 days of service of the First Amended Petition (at the latest). There being no showing of good cause to justify the late presentation, and no order authorizing late presentation, the motion is untimely. (See Code Civ. Proc., section 425.16, subd. (f); see Newport Harbor Ventures, LLC v. Morris Cerullo World Evangelism (2018) 4 Cal.5th 637, 640.) The court declines to hear the anti-SLAPP motion. While the approach of hearing a motion and denying it on procedural grounds such as untimeliness would be suitable in a more conventional procedural setting, under the plain language of the statute the consequence of such an approach would be that the order would be rendered automatically appealable. The statute authorizes an appeal from "[an] order granting or denying a special motion to strike." (CCP section 425.16(i).) Such an appeal would automatically stay all further trial proceedings on causes of action affected by the motion. (See Varian Medical Systems, Inc. v. Delfino (2005) 35 Cal.4th 180, 195.) "This means that however unsound an anti-SLAPP motion may be, it will typically stop the entire lawsuit dead in its tracks until an appellate court completes its review." (Hewlett-Packard Co. v. Oracle Corp. (2015) 239 Cal.App.4th 11174, 1185.) To issue an order allowing such a consequence, in the absence of an event which reopens the 60-day statutory period, would not satisfy the purpose of the statute's special procedure, to facilitate the dismissal of an action early in the litigation to minimize the cost to the defendant. [Part 2 of 4]

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Special Motion To Strike Second Amended Petition (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: part 3 of 4 Even if, hypothetically, Keystone's motion had been brought timely or Keystone had obtained leave of court to file the motion late, the court would have denied Keystone's motion on the separate and independent grounds that Keystone failed to meet its threshold burden to establish that the challenged allegations and claims arise from activity protected under section 425.16. (See Code Civ. Proc., section 425.16, subd. (b)(1); Baral v. Schnitt (2016) 1 Cal.5th 376, 384.) The court looks to the relevant petition to make this determination. (See Medical Marijuana, Inc. v. ProjectCBD.com (2016) 46 Cal.App.5th 869.) Important is the unique context in which Trammell's claims against Keystone arise. Like its predecessors, the Second Amended Petition was filed in an estate proceeding under Probate Code sections 850 and 859 only, meaning that any claim presented must be about the return of property to the estate/trust and possible statutory penalties. Here, Trammell claims that Keystone has $50,647.85 that must be returned to the estate. The basis of Trammell's claim is Keystone's possession of the money. Possession of money is not protected activity under section 425.16, subdivisions (e)(1) - (e)(4) ("section 425.16(e)"). This may explain why Keystone couldn't identify-and the court couldn't find-a single Section 850 case in which an anti-SLAPP motion succeeded. Keystone defends itself by contending that it earned the $50,647.85 representing Porchia and that work included petitioning activity (e.g., court filings). But the court looks to the allegations in the petition - not potential defenses - to determine whether the claims arises from protected activity. Trammell also claims that Keystone breached a fiduciary duty owed to the trust when it failed to exercise prudent actions, provide trust information, provide notice of successor trustee, inform beneficiaries and provide certain information required under the Probate Code. Inaction, especially inaction as to the internal affairs of a private trust, is not protected activity under section 425.16(e). (See Second Amended Petition at pp. 9-10, 28, and Moore v. Shaw (2004) 116 Cal.App.4th 182, 195-197.) Any allegation referring to Keystone's actions in court-and the court has found none-would be incidental to the claim based essentially on nonprotected activity. (See Hylton v. Frank E. Rogozienski, Inc. (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 164, 1275.) Of course, this analysis does not address whether Keystone owed a fiduciary duty to Trammell as trust beneficiary, whether any duty was breached or whether a breach of trust claim belongs in a section 850 petition filed in an estate proceeding; these are issues for the second step of the anti-SLAPP analysis, which the court cannot come to unless and until the first step in the analysis is satisfied. Finally, Trammell alleges that Keystone acted in concert with the other respondents; but, given the fact this claim is presented in a section 850 petition, the court construes it as focusing on the respondents' collective withholding of funds related to the sale of 64 Potomac Street, which, again, is not protected activity under section 425.16(e).

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Special Motion And Special Motion To Strike (Ccp Section 425.16) Second Amended Petition Under Probate Code Sections 850, 859
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4] This tentative is issued by Hon. Joseph M. Quinn The Court GRANTS Respondent and Moving Party Keystone Law Group's unopposed Request for Judicial Notice. On its own noticed motion, the court takes judicial notice of the following pleadings from this action: (1) Petition to Obtain Possession of Real and Personal Property Under Probate Code sections 850, 859, filed Feb. 26, 2019 ("Petition" or "Initial Petition"); (1) Amendment to Petition to Obtain Possession of Real and Personal Property Under Probate Code sections 850, 859, filed Apr. 24, 2019; (3) First Amended Petition to Obtain Possession of Real and Personal Property Under Probate Code sections 850, 859, filed Aug. 1, 2019 ("First Amended Petition"); and, (4) Second Amended Petition to Obtain Possession of Real and Personal Property Under Probate Code sections 850, 859, filed Feb. 6, 2020 ("Second Amended Petition"). Notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) Petitioner Donald Trammell is the disabled adult child of decedent Genette Trammell. He brings this action by and through his Guardian Ad Litem Clinton Killian. Trammell alleges under Probate Code section 850 that he is a beneficiary of Decedent's estate and either decedent died having a claim to real property known as 64 Potomac Street and the property or some interest therein (such as the sale proceeds) is claimed by others, including Keystone, or decedent died having a claim to 64 Potomac Street, title to or possession of which is claimed by others, including Keystone. Trammell requests that the court order that Keystone disgorge to the estate the property it holds (see Prob. C., section 850, subds. (a)(2)(C)&(D)) and that respondents, including Keystone, be ordered to pay double damages, attorney's fees and costs under Probate Code section 859. Trammell alleges that Lawrence Porchia, acting as trustee of a related trust, sold 64 Potomac Street to third parties. Keystone, for its part, is a law firm that represented Porchia for some period of time after the sale and allegedly collected fees of $50,647.85, the sum of which, according to Trammell, were proceeds from the sale of 64 Potomac Street. [Part 1 of 4]
